data.title: ビジネスメール（依頼）
data.text: Hi Mei. Thank you for your message. I was wondering if you could send me the latest figures by Friday. We have a meeting with the client on Monday morning. Please let me know if you need anything from my side. I really appreciate your help. Best regards, Ken.

data.title: ごみ分別の案内
data.text: Welcome to Midori Heights. Please separate your garbage into four types: burnable, plastic, cans and bottles, and paper. Burnable garbage is collected on Tuesday and Friday mornings. Please put it out by eight o'clock. Plastic goes out on Wednesdays. Cans and bottles are collected every second Saturday. Old newspapers and cardboard should be tied with string and placed by the entrance on Mondays. If you miss a collection day, please keep the garbage inside your room. Do not leave bags in the hallway. Thank you for keeping our building clean.

data.title: エレベーター停止のお知らせ
data.text: Notice to all residents: The east elevator will be stopped for its yearly safety inspection on Thursday, March 14th, from 9 a.m. to 4 p.m. During this time, please use the west elevator or the stairs. The inspection team may also test the alarm bell around noon, so you may hear a short ringing sound. This is only a test. If you are planning to move large furniture on that day, we recommend changing your schedule. We are sorry for the inconvenience and thank you for your understanding.

data.title: 車内アナウンス
data.text: Thank you for riding the Blue Line today. This train is bound for Central Station. The next stop is River Park. The doors on the left side will open. Please stand clear of the closing doors. If you are standing, please hold on to a strap or a handrail. Priority seats are located near the doors for elderly passengers, expectant mothers, and passengers with disabilities. Please set your mobile phone to silent mode and refrain from talking on the phone. The next stop is River Park. Thank you.

data.title: カフェの本日のメニュー
data.text: Welcome to Cafe Sora! Today's lunch special is grilled chicken with rice and a small salad for 950 yen. It comes with a free drink: coffee, tea, or orange juice. Our soup of the day is pumpkin soup. For dessert, we recommend the apple pie, baked fresh this morning. It is 400 yen, or 300 yen if you order it with a lunch set. We are open from 11 a.m. to 9 p.m. Last order is at 8:30 p.m. Please note that we are closed on Wednesdays. Thank you, and enjoy your meal!

data.title: 図書館の利用案内
data.text: Welcome to the City Library. You can borrow up to ten books for two weeks. To get a library card, please bring something that shows your name and address, such as a driver's license or a utility bill. Books can be returned at the counter or in the return box by the main entrance, which is open 24 hours. If a book you want is on loan, you can reserve it online, and we will email you when it is ready. Please do not bring food or drinks into the reading rooms. Bottled water is allowed.

data.title: 忘れ物センターからの電話
data.text: Hello, this is the Lost and Found Center at Green Line Station. We received your inquiry about a black backpack. Good news—we believe we have it. It was found on the 8:15 train this morning. Inside, there is a blue water bottle, a laptop, and a small notebook, just as you described. You can pick it up at our office on the second floor of the station. We are open every day from 9 a.m. to 7 p.m. Please bring a photo ID. We will keep the item for thirty days. Thank you.

data.title: 傘の忘れ物ランキング
data.text: Every year, the Tokyo Metro finds about 300,000 lost umbrellas. That is roughly one umbrella for every 45 passengers. Most are found on rainy Monday mornings, when commuters rush to catch their trains and forget what they were holding. Only about 1 in 20 lost umbrellas is ever claimed. The rest are kept for a set period and then donated, recycled, or sold cheaply at station events. Staff say the easiest way to avoid losing yours is simple: attach a small tag with your name, or choose a bright color that stands out from the sea of black umbrellas on every train.

data.title: 自転車通勤のすすめ
data.text: More people are choosing to cycle to work, and it's easy to see why. A 20-minute bike ride burns roughly the same calories as a light jog, without feeling like exercise. It also skips traffic jams entirely—many cyclists report shorter commute times than drivers on the same route during rush hour. To get started safely, check your local bike lane map, wear a helmet, and use lights if you'll be riding before sunrise or after sunset. Many offices now offer secure bike parking and even showers, so you can arrive fresh and ready for the day.

data.title: 駐車場の精算案内
data.text: Thank you for parking with us. Please take your ticket from the machine at the entrance and keep it with you. Rates are 200 yen for the first 30 minutes and 100 yen for each additional 30 minutes. The maximum charge per day is 1,500 yen. Before returning to your car, please pay at one of the payment machines located near the elevators, and insert your validated ticket into the exit gate within 15 minutes. We accept cash and all major credit cards. Lost ticket fee is a flat 2,000 yen. Have a safe drive.

data.title: 商品交換の案内
data.text: If the item you received doesn't fit or isn't quite right, exchanges are easy. Bring the item, its tags, and your receipt to any of our stores within 30 days, and our staff will help you find the right size or a different color, free of charge. If you'd rather shop online, print the prepaid return label from your order page, attach it to the box, and drop it off at any post office. Your new item will ship as soon as we receive the returned one, usually within three business days. Sale items can be exchanged but not refunded.

data.title: ギフトカードのご利用案内
data.text: Thank you for your gift card! Here's what you need to know: the card never expires and there are no hidden fees. You can use it online or in-store, and it can be combined with sale prices or other gift cards. If your purchase costs less than the card's balance, the remaining amount stays on the card for future use. If it costs more, you can pay the difference with another card or cash. To check your balance at any time, visit our website and enter the 16-digit code on the back of your card, or simply ask a cashier in-store.

data.title: 隣人への挨拶状
data.text: Hi everyone, we just moved into apartment 302 last week. My name is Yuki, and I live here with my partner and our small dog, Mochi, who is very friendly but barks a little when the doorbell rings—sorry in advance! We work from home most days, so if you ever need someone to receive a package or water your plants while you're away, feel free to knock. We're looking forward to getting to know the building. Thanks for reading, and see you around!

data.title: カーペットの染み抜きガイド
data.text: Act fast—the sooner you treat a stain, the easier it comes out. First, blot (don't rub) the spill with a clean cloth to remove as much liquid as possible; rubbing pushes the stain deeper into the fibers. For most stains, mix one tablespoon of dish soap with two cups of warm water, dab it onto the stain, and blot again. For coffee or wine, a small amount of white vinegar mixed with water works well. Always test any cleaning solution on a hidden part of the carpet first, and let the area air dry completely before walking on it.

data.title: 食洗機の効率的な使い方
data.text: Getting the most out of your dishwasher isn't complicated, but a few habits make a big difference. Scrape off large food scraps, but don't bother pre-rinsing everything—most modern dishwashers are designed to handle light food residue, and pre-rinsing just wastes water. Load plates facing the center where the spray arm is strongest, and keep bowls and cups tilted downward so water doesn't pool inside them. Run the dishwasher only when it's full; half-empty loads waste both water and electricity. Once a month, run an empty cycle with a cup of vinegar to clear mineral buildup and odors.

data.title: 観葉植物とオフィスの空気
data.text: Adding a few plants to your desk does more than brighten the space. Studies show that office workers near greenery report lower stress and slightly better concentration than those in plant-free environments. Low-maintenance options are best for busy offices: pothos and snake plants tolerate irregular watering and low light, making them nearly impossible to kill. If natural light is limited, a small LED grow light can keep plants healthy without taking up much space. Even a single small plant on a shared desk has been shown to lift the mood of everyone nearby, not just its owner.

data.title: 傘をなくした話
data.text: I lost my umbrella on the train yesterday. I remember opening it in the morning because it was raining hard. When I got off at my station, the rain had stopped, so I closed the umbrella and set it next to my bag on the seat. I stood up, grabbed my bag, and walked off. Two hours later I realized my hand was empty. I called the lost-and-found office, but nobody had turned it in. I bought a cheap plastic one at the convenience store on the way home. It works, but it's not the same. I really liked that umbrella.

data.title: 鍵を家に忘れた朝
data.text: I left my house keys on the kitchen table this morning. I only noticed after the door had already locked behind me. Luckily, my roommate was still home. I knocked, waited, and she opened the door with a sleepy smile. She said this was the third time this month. I laughed, but she was right. I need to fix this habit. Today after work, I'm going to buy one of those small trackers you can attach to a keychain. My phone will tell me where the keys are. It's a small thing, but it will save me a lot of stress.

data.title: 近所のドッグパーク
data.text: There is a small dog park near my apartment. I go there every Saturday morning with my dog, Buddy. He is a friendly brown mix, and he loves running with the other dogs. Most of the same people come each week, so I have started to know some of them. There is a woman with two little white dogs, and an older man with a big black dog named Duke. We don't talk much, but we say hello, and Buddy sniffs Duke, and Duke sniffs Buddy. It's a quiet, easy way to start the weekend.

data.title: お気に入りのカフェ
data.text: There is a small café two blocks from my office. I go there almost every morning. The coffee is good, but the real reason I go is the woman who works there. She remembers what I order. When I walk in, she smiles and says, 'Medium latte, right?' I say yes, and she starts making it before I finish paying. It only saves me thirty seconds, but it makes the morning feel warmer. Big chain coffee shops are faster and cheaper, but no barcode scanner will ever remember my face.

data.title: 火災報知器の電池
data.text: The smoke detector in the hallway started beeping at two in the morning last night. Just one short beep, every thirty seconds. I got up, stood on a chair, and pressed every button I could find. The beeping continued. Finally I remembered: it wasn't a fire. It was the battery. The device beeps to tell you it's dying. I didn't have a new battery in the house, so I took the whole thing off the ceiling and put it in a drawer. I bought new batteries the next morning. The safe thing to do would have been to buy them the same night. I got lucky.

data.title: 自転車のパンク
data.text: My bike had a flat tire this morning. I saw it as soon as I opened the garage. The back tire was completely flat. I didn't have time to fix it before work, so I took the bus. On the way home, I stopped at the bike shop near the station. The man there looked at the tire for about ten seconds and said, 'A small nail. Easy fix.' Twenty minutes later, my bike was ready. It cost ten dollars. I was surprised it was so cheap. Now I want to learn how to fix it myself next time.

data.title: 冷蔵庫の中身
data.text: My refrigerator is full of leftovers again. I cook big meals on Sunday, and by Wednesday I usually get tired of eating the same thing. Today I opened three containers before I found one I still wanted. My mother used to say, 'Eat what you have before you buy more.' She was right, but I don't always listen. This weekend, I'm going to try something different. I will cook two different dishes, one for the first half of the week and one for the second half. Maybe that will keep meals interesting until Sunday.

data.title: 朝のジム通い
data.text: I started going to the gym before work last month. My alarm rings at 5:30, and I hate it every single morning. But once I'm at the gym, I'm always glad I came. It's quiet at that time, only three or four other people. I finish by 7:00 and get to the office by 8:30, feeling awake and calm. On days I skip the gym, I actually feel more tired. That's strange, but true. I think the hard part is only the first ten minutes. If I can just get out of bed, the rest takes care of itself.

data.title: 隣の家の猫
data.text: My neighbor's cat visits my balcony almost every day. His name is Mochi, and he is orange and white. My neighbor told me not to feed him, so I never do. But I do leave a small bowl of water in the summer. Mochi drinks a little, sits in the sun for maybe an hour, and then walks back over the fence to his own house. He never lets me touch him. He just wants a quiet place to rest. I don't mind. It's nice to have a small orange visitor who doesn't want anything but water and sunshine.

data.title: お弁当を作る習慣
data.text: I started bringing lunch from home about six months ago. In the beginning, I only did it because I wanted to save money. Buying lunch every day at work was costing me almost 300 dollars a month. Now I actually enjoy making my lunch. I usually cook a little extra at dinner and put it in a container for the next day. My coworkers sometimes ask what I brought, and now several of them have started doing the same thing. I'm not a great cook, but a simple homemade meal is almost always better than an expensive sandwich from the shop downstairs.

data.title: 引っ越し用の箱を集める
data.text: I am moving next month, and I need a lot of boxes. Buying them at the moving store is expensive, so I asked at the grocery store near my house. The manager was very kind. He said they get new deliveries on Tuesday and Thursday mornings, and I could take any empty boxes I wanted after seven o'clock. I went last Thursday and got fifteen good, clean boxes for free. I only had to break them down at home and tape them back together. If you are moving soon, just ask. Most stores are happy to give away boxes they would only throw out.

data.title: 目覚ましを止め忘れる
data.text: I forgot to turn off my alarm on Saturday. It rang at 6:00 a.m., which is my weekday time. I was so tired that I didn't even open my eyes. I just reached out and hit the snooze button, and again, and again. By 6:30 I was fully awake and confused. Then I remembered it was the weekend. I could have slept for three more hours. I got up anyway because I was already awake. It turned into a nice morning. I made coffee slowly, read a book, and watched the light come through the window. Maybe waking up early on weekends isn't always bad.
